# Passage: 10
A 2-year-old boy presented with short stature, weight loss, lactose intolerance, excessive flatulence, foul-smelling and fatty stools, and other intestinal symptoms. A biopsy of the boy’s small intestine and specific antibody tests led the attending physician to believe it was likely that the child had celiac disease. To confirm the diagnosis, the physician placed the boy on a strict gluten-free diet.  
Found in wheat and certain other grains, glutens are a type of protein that contains proline-rich regions that the human body cannot digest. In people who have celiac disease, these indigestible polypeptides are taken up by the mucosal epithelium of the small intestine where they are deaminated by the enzyme TG2. The deaminated polypeptide often triggers an immunological inflammatory response that damages the lining of the small intestine. The intestinal villi atrophy, leading to a variety of symptoms, including those indicative of malnutrition.  
Within days of being started on a gluten-free diet, the boy began to have an improvement in symptoms. After just a few months, a small-intestinal biopsy showed normal morphology and the boy experienced a growth spurt.  
---
